title: RX buffer overflow: shim writes up to 64B into a caller canRead buffer (classic 8B / non-FD-opened channel)
description: write_read_outputs (src/lib.rs:336) copies f.len bytes into the app's canRead/canReadWait msg buffer with NO caller-supplied length. f.len is peer-controlled, bounded only to 64 by the decoders. Two triggers: (1) a legitimate CAN FD frame (up to 64B) delivered to a channel opened WITHOUT canOPEN_CAN_FD, whose app sizes msg at 8B per the classic Kvaser contract; (2) an adversarial non-FD ('classic') frame with len 9..64 -- both decoders only reject len>64, never enforcing the classic <=8 invariant (src/wire.rs:221 decode_stream, :303 parse_udp). Up to 56B OOB write in the host app; network-triggerable (UDP source spoofable even with peercheck on). The project's own selftest already overflows: canshim_probe.c opens with flags=0 and reads FD frames into rbuf[8] while the FD case injects a 16B frame -- it passes only by luck. Fix: (a) decoders reject non-FD len>8; (b) honor canOPEN_CAN_FD on canOpenChannel and cap classic-channel RX delivery to 8B (clear FDF); (c) update probe to open canOPEN_CAN_FD + rbuf[64] for FD; add unit tests + reverify selftest. Found via adversarial multi-agent review 2026-06-26.
close_reason: Fixed + verified. (1) wire.rs decoders reject non-FD frames with len>8 (CLASSIC_FRAME_MAX_LEN); (2) Conn.fd_capable set from canOPEN_CAN_FD in canOpenChannel; write_read_outputs caps classic-channel RX delivery to 8 bytes and reports classic semantics, so a 64B FD frame can't overflow an 8B buffer; (3) probe opens canOPEN_CAN_FD + rbuf[64] for FD. 4 new unit tests + selftest PASS on 32/64-bit (incl FD+BRS via the FD-capable open path). Also clamped canGetNumberOfChannels to i32::MAX (agent finding).

title: canWrite can wedge the whole DLL: TCP write has no timeout and runs under the global CONNS lock
description: write_frame (src/lib.rs:234-238) holds the global CONNS mutex across c.write(&f). The TCP write path (transport.rs:355-359) is stream.write_all() on a socket with NO write timeout (connect() only sets nodelay, transport.rs:302). If the Linux peer stalls (cannelloni paused, slow vcan, blackhole), the kernel send buffer fills and write_all blocks indefinitely while holding the global lock -> every other channel's canRead/canWrite/canClose/canOpenChannel blocks too. Defeats the per-channel isolation of the handle table (kvasilloni-j83); hangs even single-channel apps with a watchdog thread; bites the TCP mode the README recommends for NMEA 2000. Everywhere else the code drops the global lock before blocking; this is the one place it does not.
acceptance_criteria: Set SO_SNDTIMEO on the TCP stream; release CONNS before the network write (Arc<Conn> in the map); a stalled peer makes canWrite return an error instead of hanging, and other channels keep working. On a TCP write error mark the connection not-ready so the desynced stream is not reused.
close_reason: canWrite clones the channel Arc and drops CONNS before the (possibly blocking) send; TCP socket gets set_write_timeout(connect_timeout) and a write error tears the conn down. Conn is now Arc<Conn> in the map with &self methods. No more whole-DLL wedge on a stalled TCP peer.

title: canClose from inside a canSetNotify callback self-joins RX thread -> deadlock
description: The notify callback registered via canSetNotify runs ON the RX thread (Shared::push, transport.rs:111-119). If the app's callback calls canClose (a very common 'close on error' idiom), canClose -> Conn::close -> handle.join() joins the RX thread from within the RX thread itself, which deadlocks forever, freezing whatever thread the app later waits on. Fix: record the RX thread id; in close(), if std::thread::current().id() == rx thread id, signal/stop without joining (let it unwind), otherwise join as today.
acceptance_criteria: Calling canClose from within a notify callback returns without deadlock; covered by a test that closes from the callback.
close_reason: close() compares JoinHandle thread id to current thread; detaches instead of self-joining when invoked from the RX thread (notify callback).

title: canClose does not wake threads blocked in canReadWait/canReadSync (multi-thread hang)
description: Conn::close (transport.rs:323) sets running=false, shuts the socket, and joins the RX thread, but never signals Shared.cv. A second thread blocked in canReadWait/canReadSync is parked on that condvar via pop_wait/peek_wait (transport.rs:130,141) and is only released by its own timeout - up to ~49 days for canWAIT_INFINITE. Common pattern (one reader thread + one control thread that closes on shutdown) therefore hangs at teardown. Fix: add a 'closed' AtomicBool to Shared, set it in close(), call cv.notify_all(), and make the wait predicates also return when closed.
acceptance_criteria: A thread blocked in canReadWait(INFINITE) returns promptly (CAN_ERR_NOMSG) when another thread calls canClose; covered by a test.
close_reason: Added Shared.closed flag + mark_closed(); close() wakes blocked pop_wait/peek_wait via notify_all; tests confirm prompt wake on close.

title: RX parser panics on overlong CAN length byte -> permanent silent RX death
description: parse_udp (wire.rs:284) and decode_stream Data state (wire.rs:227) copy 'len & 0x7F' bytes (up to 127) into Frame.data, which is only [u8;64]. A length byte of 65..127 indexes out of bounds and panics. The panic happens on the RX background thread (udp_rx_loop/tcp_rx_loop), which is NOT wrapped in catch_unwind, so the thread dies permanently and silently: canRead returns CAN_ERR_NOMSG forever and the app hangs waiting for data. UDP is unauthenticated, so any host on the network (or a buggy/old cannelloni peer) can trigger it with a single datagram. VERIFIED empirically: temporary probe tests produced 'range end index 100 out of range for slice of length 64' (parse_udp) and 'range end index 70 ...' (decode_stream). Fix: validate (len & 0x7F) <= 64 in both decoders and reject as None/Decoded::Error otherwise.
acceptance_criteria: parse_udp returns None and decode_stream returns Decoded::Error for any frame whose data length field exceeds 64; regression tests cover len=65..127 for both UDP and TCP paths; RX thread survives a malformed packet.
close_reason: Both decoders reject len>64 (MAX_FRAME_LEN) at the copy chokepoints; regression tests cover len 65..127 for UDP and TCP; RX thread survives malformed packets.

title: canlib32 shim: 13 exports + UDP/TCP cannelloni transport
description: Drop-in canlib32.dll implementing the 13 resolved Kvaser symbols; winsock RX thread + ring; UDP datagram and TCP (CANNELLONIv1 handshake) transports; env config.
close_reason: Shim implements all 13 exports + UDP and TCP cannelloni transports; builds clean 32/64-bit

title: cannelloni wire codec + host interop test
description: Pure-C codec (encodeFrame/decodeFrame, UDP buildPacket/parsePacket) + Kvaser<->SocketCAN id translation, compiling under both mingw and native gcc. Host test cross-validates against cannelloni's own parser.cpp/decoder.cpp.
close_reason: Codec done; cross-validated vs cannelloni's own parser.cpp/decoder.cpp in all 4 directions, RESULT: PASS

title: R3: Race-detect the transport concurrency (Shared ring + Conn teardown)
description:
PROBLEM
The transport concurrency (Shared: Mutex<VecDeque>+Condvar+cb_active; Conn close
join-vs-detach) is the source of 4vc/cqe/hc9/lo7. im6 added deterministic tests
(disarm-drains-inflight, reentrant-no-deadlock) but no systematic race detection.

INSTRUCTIONS (pick the available tooling; document the choice)
- Preferred: run the transport tests under ThreadSanitizer:
    RUSTFLAGS="-Zsanitizer=thread" cargo +nightly test -Zbuild-std ... transport
  Fix or annotate anything it flags. Add the invocation to docs/CI.
- Alternative: model the Shared ring + notify path with `loom` (push/pop/
  set_notify/mark_closed interleavings) and assert no UAF/lost-wakeup/deadlock
  across all schedules.
- Concretely target: (a) push() firing the callback while another thread calls
  set_notify(0,0,0) - the cb_active drain must serialize them (4vc); (b) a
  callback that calls close() on the RX thread - detach not self-join (cqe);
  (c) a reader in pop_wait/peek_wait that mark_closed() must wake (hc9).
- Fallback if no nightly/loom: a higher-contention std-thread stress test
  (many producers + concurrent set_notify/close) run for a bounded duration,
  plus the existing selftest --stress; document that this is a probabilistic
  stand-in, not a proof.

ACCEPTANCE
- TSan-clean OR loom-clean for the targeted primitives; OR a documented stress
  stand-in is added. Removing a lock/drain (temporarily) is detected by the
  chosen method.
acceptance_criteria: The Shared/Conn concurrency tests run race-clean under ThreadSanitizer (nightly -Zsanitizer=thread) OR the Shared ring + notify drain are modeled with loom and pass. Specifically exercises the cb_active drain (4vc), close join-vs-detach (cqe), and condvar wakeup (hc9). If neither tool is available, a documented heavier std-thread stress regime stands in and is added to the suite.
close_reason:
Chose ThreadSanitizer (nightly -Zsanitizer=thread -Z build-std, instrumented std). loom rejected: would require cfg(loom) swaps of std sync primitives in production code and cannot model the real JoinHandle/thread::current().id() close() self-join (cqe).
- Installed rust-src for nightly; whole transport module (29 existing + 3 new tests) runs TSan-clean.
- Added 3 targeted tests: cb_active_drain_orders_app_context_access (4vc - non-atomic app-context read in callback vs post-drain write; TSan-detectable), close_from_notify_callback_detaches_not_self_joins (cqe - real UDP Conn whose RX-thread callback calls close(), proving detach-not-join), shared_concurrent_stress_is_race_clean (4 producers + notify churner + blocking readers woken by mark_closed; doubles as the documented std-thread stress stand-in for hosts without TSan). Used a dedicated cb_noop, not cb_silent, to avoid the im6.1 shared-counter bleed.
- VERIFIED detection: removing the set_notify cb_active drain makes TSan report a data race on the context (Read of size 8 by RX thread T2 racing the write); with TSAN_OPTIONS=halt_on_error=1 it is a hard 'error: test failed'.
- Documented recipe: 'make race' target + the race-detection comment block in transport.rs. Restored the drain; suite green (82 tests).

title: R1: Property-test the wire codec (encode/decode round-trip + never-panic on arbitrary bytes)
description:
PROBLEM
wire.rs (parse_udp / decode_stream / encode_frame) is the untrusted-input parse
surface and the source of kkt/nmt/56p. im6 added POINT tests for known malformed
cases; there is no PROPERTY coverage proving the invariants hold for the whole
input space.

INSTRUCTIONS
Add property tests (prefer the `proptest` dev-dependency; if the host is offline,
hand-roll a bounded pseudo-random generator seeded per-iteration - no dep). Cover:
- Round-trip identity: for an arbitrary VALID Frame (random can_id incl. EFF/RTR
  bits, fd flag, valid fd_flags, len within the class limit, random data),
  encode_frame -> decode_stream reconstructs an equal frame; and build_udp ->
  parse_udp reconstructs it. Classic vs FD vs RTR all covered.
- Never-panic / no-OOB: for an ARBITRARY &[u8] (any length, any bytes),
  parse_udp(buf) and the decode_stream state machine driven over the bytes both
  return without panicking and without reading past the buffer (catch_unwind to
  assert no unwind; rely on debug-assertions bounds checks).
- Length-class invariant: any frame parse_udp/decode_stream RETURNS satisfies
  len<=8 when !fd and len<=64 when fd (no over-length frame ever escapes).
- udp_prealloc_cap invariant: result <= buf_len/FRAME_BASE_SIZE + 1 for all inputs.

ACCEPTANCE
- Round-trip + never-panic + length-class properties green under cargo test.
- Dropping a length guard in wire.rs (temporarily) makes a property fail.
- Document the chosen approach (proptest vs hand-rolled) in the test module.
acceptance_criteria: Round-trip property holds for arbitrary valid frames (classic/FD/RTR, all ids/lens). parse_udp and decode_stream are shown to never panic and never read out of bounds for ARBITRARY byte input. A reintroduced bounds bug (e.g. dropping a len guard) is caught by the property suite. Runs under cargo test.
close_reason:
Added proptest (dev-dependency only; not linked into the DLL) property suite to wire.rs:
- prop_tcp_roundtrip / prop_udp_roundtrip: encode_frame->decode_stream and build_udp->parse_udp are identity for arbitrary VALID frames (classic/FD/RTR, every id and in-class length, valid fd_flags).
- prop_parse_udp_never_panics / prop_decode_stream_never_panics: catch_unwind proves no panic/OOB on arbitrary bytes; every returned frame obeys the length-class invariant (len<=8 classic, <=64 FD). parse_udp generator weights toward valid-header + small matching count so escaped frames are actually inspected (an attacker-huge count returns None all-or-nothing and would mask an escape).
- prop_udp_prealloc_cap_bounded: clamp bound holds for all (count, buf_len).
Verified acceptance: dropping the parse_udp class limit makes prop_parse_udp_never_panics fail (shrinks to classic len=9); weakening the decode_stream classic guard fails prop_decode_stream_never_panics. Holds at 8192 cases. proptest-regressions/ gitignored.

title: Robustness & race-condition hardening (fuzz parser + race-detect transport)
description:
Apply GENERATIVE tooling (property tests, fuzzing, race detection) to the two
bug-dense areas of this network-facing CAN shim - the untrusted wire parser and
the transport concurrency - plus two areas epic kvasilloni-im6 did not cover:
error-path resource leaks and the config.rs INI/env parser.

## Audit rationale
The recent fix history clusters in exactly the two highest-risk areas:
  - Untrusted-parse overflows: kkt (len>64 over-read), nmt (classic 8-byte cap),
    56p (count-driven over-allocation). parse_udp/decode_stream consume raw bytes
    straight off the wire.
  - Transport concurrency UAF/deadlock: 4vc (notify use-after-free), cqe
    (close-from-callback self-join), hc9 (lost wakeup), lo7 (TCP write teardown).
Epic im6 added POINT tests for the known cases. This epic applies GENERATIVE
tools - proptest, cargo-fuzz, ThreadSanitizer/loom - to surface the cases point
tests miss, and extends coverage to error-path leaks and config parsing.

Do children in priority order (P2 first). Each is independently shippable.

## Network / toolchain caveat (READ FIRST)
proptest, cargo-fuzz, a nightly toolchain, and ThreadSanitizer all require crates
or toolchains fetched over the network; Cargo.toml is std-only today and the host
has no coverage/fuzz tooling installed. Confirm the host (and any CI) can fetch
them before starting R1/R2/R3. Fallbacks if offline: R1 can use a hand-rolled
small-input generator (no dep); R3 can fall back to heavier std-thread stress +
the selftest --stress regime instead of TSan/loom. Record whichever path is taken.

## ONBOARDING (clean start in a new session)
Project: kvasilloni - a Rust cdylib building a Windows canlib32.dll, a drop-in
Kvaser CANlib replacement that bridges a Windows CAN app to a Linux vcan over the
network by speaking the cannelloni wire protocol (UDP packet-framed, or TCP
headerless stream) directly to a stock cannelloni peer.

Source layout (src/):
  - lib.rs       : extern "system" (stdcall) FFI exports, the CONNS handle table,
                   out-param marshalling (write_read_outputs), guard()/guard_void().
  - transport.rs : Conn (one channel), background RX thread + bounded ring (Shared:
                   Mutex<VecDeque>+Condvar), UDP/TCP sockets, peer-IP filtering, the
                   canSetNotify callback (cb_active drain), close() join-vs-detach.
  - wire.rs      : the cannelloni codec (build_udp/parse_udp, encode_frame/
                   decode_stream, udp_prealloc_cap) + Kvaser<->SocketCAN translation.
  - config.rs    : layered config (defaults < kvasilloni.ini < KVASILLONI_* env),
                   parse_num warnings, IPv6 host, allow-list parsing.

Tests:
  - Unit/integration tests inline under #[cfg(test)] in each src/*.rs. Run:
    `cargo test` (currently 74 tests, host build / Linux). transport.rs already has
    fast loopback network tests (im6.5); lib.rs has a CONNS_TEST_LOCK that
    serializes channel-owning tests against the stress test's CONNS-empty check.
  - E2E: test/selftest.sh drives the REAL DLL under wine vs stock cannelloni on an
    isolated vcan, both directions, both arches:
        SELFTEST_ARCH=32 bash test/selftest.sh
        SELFTEST_ARCH=64 bash test/selftest.sh
    Windows-side actor: test/canshim_probe.c. Rebuild DLLs after src changes
    (selftest reuses a cached DLL):
        cargo build --release --target i686-pc-windows-gnu
        cargo build --release --target x86_64-pc-windows-gnu

Host has: passwordless sudo; cannelloni, wine, can-utils, mingw, rust windows-gnu
targets; vcan0/can0/vcan1/vcan2 links. IMPORTANT: vcan0 hosts unrelated research -
do NOT disturb it; the selftest uses vcan1 (+vcan2 for multi-rx). For loopback
unit tests bind 127.0.0.1 ephemeral sockets (127.0.0.2 is a usable 2nd source IP).
NO coverage/fuzz tooling and NO clippy are installed.

Released: v0.3.0. The im6 test-hardening batch is on main.

Children enumerate each work item with concrete instructions + acceptance:
  R1 proptest the wire codec
  R2 cargo-fuzz harness for parse_udp + decode_stream
  R3 race-detect Shared/Conn (ThreadSanitizer or loom)
  R4 error-path resource-leak audit (TCP handshake-fail, UDP bind-fail)
  R5 config.rs INI/env robustness (property/fuzz the parser)
acceptance_criteria: All children closed; cargo test green; selftest PASS on 32- and 64-bit; the wire parser survives a fuzz/property run with no panic/UB; the transport concurrency primitives are race-clean (ThreadSanitizer or loom) or, if that tooling is unavailable, covered by a documented heavier stress regime; no fd/thread leak across many failed opens.

title: Tighten selftest RX assertions to verify payload/dlc/flags, not just id
description:
selftest RX checks assert only the frame id - tighten to verify payload/dlc/flags.

PROBLEM
Nearly every RXGREP in test/selftest.sh is "RX id=0x..." only; a receive path
that delivered the WRONG bytes would still pass. The FD+BRS case asserts a single
flag bit but never dlc>8 or the 16-byte payload, so wide-payload FD RECEIVE is not
actually verified end-to-end. This is the single highest-leverage e2e improvement.

INSTRUCTIONS
- The probe already prints "RX id=... dlc=... flag=... data=<hex>". Update the
  selftest RXGREP patterns so each RX case asserts the injected payload hex (and,
  where relevant, dlc and flag), anchored to one line - not just the id.
- Classic cases: assert the cansend payload (e.g. id=0x18FF0102 data=01020304 ->
  RXGREP includes data=01020304).
- FD+BRS case: assert dlc equals the (rounded) injected length and the full data
  hex, in addition to the FD/BRS flag bits.
- Re-run both arches; rebuild DLLs first if src changed.

ACCEPTANCE
- Every RX case asserts id AND payload (and dlc/flag for FD).
- A deliberately corrupted payload in a delivered frame fails the relevant case.
- selftest PASS on SELFTEST_ARCH=32 and =64.
close_reason: Tightened every default-mode RX case to assert id+dlc+flag+payload (classic UDP/TCP, FD+BRS now dlc=16+full 16-byte payload+0x30004 flags, INI, malformed-survival, peer-check, TCP server). Added data= output to probe --accept/--multi/--multi-rx and tightened those positives to assert payload (AA, CD, A1, B1); cross-leak negatives kept id-only. PASS on 32 and 64.

title: Add e2e test: classic-opened channel caps a real FD frame at 8 bytes
description:
No end-to-end test for the core overflow-fix guarantee (kvasilloni-nmt): a
classic-opened channel must cap a real FD frame at 8 bytes.

PROBLEM
The safety property is only unit-tested via write_read_outputs
(src/lib.rs ~:classic_channel_caps_rx_to_eight_bytes). Every e2e FD injection in
selftest.sh targets a channel opened WITH canOPEN_CAN_FD. There is no e2e case
where a real >8-byte FD frame on the wire is delivered to a channel opened CLASSIC
(flags=0, 8-byte buffer) - which is exactly the path the fix protects.

INSTRUCTIONS
- In test/canshim_probe.c add a mode --classic-rx <hexid> <std|ext>: open the
  channel with flags=0 (classic), use unsigned char rbuf[8], canBusOn, poll
  canRead for a few seconds, print each RX as "RX id=... dlc=... flag=... data=".
- In test/selftest.sh add a CASE (UDP, on an FD-capable vcan): start cannelloni,
  run the probe in --classic-rx mode, then `cansend <vcan> <id>##1<16+ data
  bytes>` (a BRS FD frame). Assert: the probe stays alive and exits cleanly (no
  crash), reports dlc<=8, and does NOT report an FD flag bit (flag<0x10000).
- Rebuild both DLLs before running (selftest reuses cached DLLs).

ACCEPTANCE
- New selftest case PASS on SELFTEST_ARCH=32 and =64.
- Reverting the write_read_outputs classic cap (lib.rs) makes the case fail/crash.
close_reason: Added probe --classic-rx mode (CLASSIC open, rbuf[8]) + a selftest UDP case injecting a 16-byte BRS FD frame to a classic-opened channel. Asserts: probe exits cleanly AND the delivered frame is capped to dlc=8, classic flags (0x4, no FD bit), first-8 payload. Verified attributable: reverting the write_read_outputs cap -> SELFTEST: FAIL (dlc=16/payload mismatch; wine tolerated the rbuf overflow without crashing, so the capped-payload sub-check is what catches it). PASS on 32 and 64.

title: Add unit test for multi-frame UDP datagrams (parse_udp count>1)
description:
No test exercises multi-frame UDP datagrams (parse_udp count>1).

PROBLEM
parse_udp's `for _ in 0..count` loop (src/wire.rs ~:297) is never tested with
count>1: build_udp only emits count=1 and `cansend` sends one frame per datagram.
Stock cannelloni BATCHES multiple frames per UDP datagram under load, so the real
RX path - per-frame pointer advancement, mixed classic/FD/RTR batching - has zero
coverage. An off-by-one in the loop offset would ship undetected.

INSTRUCTIONS
In src/wire.rs tests, add a test-only helper to build a count-N datagram (header
{ver=2, op=DATA, seq, count BE} followed by N encode_frame outputs). Then:
- pack 3 frames in one datagram: a classic frame (e.g. 0x123, 3 data bytes), an
  FD frame (FD bit + flags + 16 bytes), and an RTR frame (RTR id, no data);
  parse_udp it and assert all three decode with correct id / len / fd / fd_flags /
  data, in order.
- add a truncated-batch case: header claims count=3 but only 2 frames' bytes are
  present -> parse_udp returns None (no panic, no over-read).

ACCEPTANCE
- New test enqueues all batched frames with correct fields and ordering.
- A deliberate off-by-one in the parse_udp per-frame offset is caught by the test.
close_reason: Added build_udp_batch test helper + parse_udp_decodes_multi_frame_batch (classic+FD+RTR in one datagram, ordered/field-checked) and parse_udp_truncated_batch_returns_none. Verified an off-by-one (p+=5) in the per-frame offset fails the batch test.

title: Notify unit tests share a process-global counter (parallel-run flake)
description:
Two notify unit tests share process-global state and can race under parallel runs.

PROBLEM
notify_fires_for_each_rx_when_armed (src/transport.rs ~:929) and
notify_silent_when_flag_not_set (~:944) both drive the module-global statics
NOTIFY_COUNT / NOTIFY_LAST_ID via the shared `test_cb`. `cargo test` runs tests
in parallel threads, so one test's `NOTIFY_COUNT.store(0)` can reset the other's
counter mid-assert, or one's increments can bleed into the other. They pass today
by luck; this is a latent flake.

INSTRUCTIONS
Make the two tests independent. Preferred: give each test its own callback fn and
its own statics (e.g. cb_fires + FIRES_COUNT/FIRES_LAST_ID, cb_silent +
SILENT_COUNT) so nothing mutable is shared and parallelism is preserved.
(Alternative: a single `static NOTIFY_TEST_LOCK: Mutex<()>` locked at the top of
both tests - simpler but serializes them.) Keep the exact-count assertions.

ACCEPTANCE
- No mutable global is shared between the two tests (or they hold a common lock).
- Stress-pass: `for i in $(seq 30); do cargo test --quiet notify -- --test-threads=8 || { echo FAIL; break; }; done` is green every run.
close_reason: Gave each notify test its own callback + statics (cb_fires/FIRES_*, cb_silent/SILENT_COUNT); no shared mutable state. 30x stress at --test-threads=8 green.

title: Test-suite quality + coverage hardening (2026-06-27 audit)
description:
Test-suite quality + coverage hardening, from the 2026-06-27 audit.

## Audit summary
The suite is strong where it counts (golden wire vectors; an e2e selftest that
drives the real DLL under wine + stock cannelloni + a vcan, both directions, both
arches; targeted tests for the recent security fixes). Two structural problems:
(1) a handful of tests do not verify their named behavior, and (2) the entire
transport.rs network layer has NO fast (cargo test) coverage - regressions only
surface in the slow wine/cannelloni selftest. Plus a real parallel-run flake in
the notify tests, and e2e RX checks that assert only the frame id.

Child issues enumerate each finding with concrete instructions + acceptance.
Do them in priority order (P2 first). Each is independently shippable.

## ONBOARDING (clean start in a new session)

Project: kvasilloni - a Rust cdylib that builds a Windows `canlib32.dll`, a
drop-in replacement for Kvaser's CANlib that bridges a Windows CAN app to a Linux
`vcan` over the network by speaking the cannelloni wire protocol (UDP
packet-framed, or TCP headerless stream) directly to a stock `cannelloni` peer.

Source layout (src/):
  - lib.rs       : the extern "system" (stdcall) FFI exports (canOpenChannel,
                   canRead/canWrite, canReadWait, canIoCtl, canSetNotify, etc.),
                   the CONNS handle table (Mutex<BTreeMap<c_int, Arc<Conn>>>), and
                   the out-param marshalling (write_read_outputs).
  - transport.rs : Conn (one channel), the background RX thread + bounded ring
                   (Shared: Mutex<VecDeque>+Condvar), UDP/TCP sockets, peer-IP
                   filtering, the canSetNotify callback (fires on the RX thread,
                   guarded by cb_active), close() join-vs-detach.
  - wire.rs      : the cannelloni codec (build_udp/parse_udp, encode_frame/
                   decode_stream) and Kvaser<->SocketCAN id/flag translation.
  - config.rs    : layered config (defaults < kvasilloni.ini < KVASILLONI_* env).

Tests:
  - Unit tests are inline in each src/*.rs under #[cfg(test)] mod tests.
    Run: `cargo test` (currently 54 tests, host build / Linux).
  - E2E: test/selftest.sh drives the REAL DLL under wine against a stock
    cannelloni and an isolated vcan. Run both arches:
        SELFTEST_ARCH=32 bash test/selftest.sh
        SELFTEST_ARCH=64 bash test/selftest.sh
    Each takes ~1-2 min. The Windows-side actor is test/canshim_probe.c (modes:
    default TX/RX, --enum, --accept, --notify, --notify-close, --multi,
    --multi-rx, --timed-open, --stress).
  - Rebuild the DLLs after changing src (the selftest REUSES a cached DLL if
    present - it only rebuilds when missing):
        cargo build --release --target i686-pc-windows-gnu
        cargo build --release --target x86_64-pc-windows-gnu

This host already has: passwordless sudo; cannelloni, wine, can-utils, mingw
(i686/x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc) installed; rust targets i686/x86_64-pc-windows-gnu;
links vcan0, can0, vcan1, vcan2. IMPORTANT: vcan0 hosts unrelated research - do
NOT disturb it; the selftest uses vcan1 (and vcan2 for multi-rx). For new
loopback unit tests, bind 127.0.0.1 ephemeral sockets - no vcan/cannelloni needed.
There is NO coverage tooling installed (no llvm-tools / tarpaulin); audit by
inspection. clippy is NOT installed either.

Recent fixes that shape current behavior (context for writing tests):
  - nmt: canRead caps a classic-opened channel at 8 bytes; CAN FD receive needs
    the channel opened with canOPEN_CAN_FD (0x400). Decoders reject non-FD len>8.
  - 4vc: notify callback use-after-free fixed via cb_active drain in set_notify;
    re-entrant set_notify skips the drain (IN_NOTIFY_CB thread-local).
  - f1b: classic-RTR DLC now kept on both TCP and UDP decode.
  - 4sd: bad config values warn (Config.warnings, logged by canOpenChannel).
  - 2qh: canReadWait/canReadSync issued from inside a notify callback collapse to
    a non-blocking poll (read_wait_duration + transport::in_notify_callback).
  - frz: all extern "system" exports wrapped in guard()/guard_void().

Released: v0.3.0 (tag-triggered GitHub Actions release.yml builds both DLLs +
SHA256SUMS). The P3 hardening batch (f1b/4sd/2qh/frz) is on main but UNRELEASED.

Session-close protocol (CLAUDE.md, mandatory): bd close finished work; run
`cargo test` and the selftest if code changed; commit; `git pull --rebase`;
`git push`; verify "up to date with origin". Work is not done until pushed.

## Acceptance (epic)
All child issues closed; `cargo test` green; selftest PASS on 32-bit and 64-bit;
no remaining test that passes when its target behavior is broken.
close_reason: All 7 children closed. cargo test: 54 -> 74 tests, green + flake-checked (12x). selftest PASS on 32 and 64 bit, with new classic-FD-cap e2e case and payload-level RX assertions throughout. Each strengthened/new test verified attributable (fails when its target behavior is reverted). Net: closed the transport.rs network-layer fast-coverage gap, fixed the notify parallel-run flake, made 5 weak tests strict, added multi-frame UDP + FFI-boundary coverage, and found/fixed a real test-isolation flake (CONNS_TEST_LOCK).

title: Notify callback use-after-free window on canSetNotify(NULL) disarm
description: transport.rs Shared::push snapshots the notify cb under the lock, releases it, THEN calls cb. set_notify (canSetNotify disarm) just swaps the struct with no quiescence. Race: RX thread copies old cb/tag, releases lock; another thread calls canSetNotify(NULL) and frees the callback's context; RX thread then calls the dangling cb with freed tag -> UAF. Only canClose currently guarantees validity (it joins the RX thread). Narrow (notify is a retargeting-only extended export, kvasilloni-ur8) but real. Fix needs care: holding the notify lock across cb would deadlock re-entrant canSetNotify. Consider a generation counter / RCU-style swap, or document that disarm requires canClose. Found via adversarial review 2026-06-26.
close_reason: Fixed: Shared gained cb_active mutex held by the RX thread across reading AND invoking the notify callback; set_notify (canSetNotify disarm/replace) drains cb_active after swapping, so once it returns the old callback is no longer running and the app may free its context (no UAF). Re-entrant set_notify from within a callback (IN_NOTIFY_CB thread-local) skips the drain to avoid self-deadlock, mirroring close()'s self-thread detection. 2 new unit tests (reentrant-no-deadlock, disarm-drains-inflight); host tests 52/52; selftest PASS 32/64-bit incl --notify and --notify-close.

title: Restrict accepted peers by source IP (cannelloni -R / -p parity)
description: The shim accepts inbound traffic from anyone: UDP binds 0.0.0.0 and recv_from ignores the source (transport.rs:510); the TCP server accepts any client. cannelloni peer-checks the source IP against -R by default (-p disables). Add symmetric peer filtering so the shim only accepts datagrams/connections from the configured cannelloni host, with an off switch and an optional explicit allow-list. Applies to UDP and TCP-server; TCP-client is already point-to-point. Addresses the injection-surface half of kvasilloni-56p.
acceptance_criteria: Config: peercheck (default on; KVASILLONI_PEER_CHECK=off to disable, = cannelloni -p) restricts inbound to the configured host IP. Optional allow=<ip,ip,...> (KVASILLONI_ALLOW) sets an explicit allow-list replacing the default. UDP drops non-allowed sources; TCP server rejects non-allowed clients. Loopback selftest (host=127.0.0.1) still passes. Documented in README/ini.example with the multi-homing/NAT caveat.
close_reason: Peer-IP check (default on) restricts UDP datagrams + TCP-server accepts to host/allow-list; KVASILLONI_PEER_CHECK=off disables (=cannelloni -p). e2e selftest case proves on=drop / off=pass. IPv4+IPv6.

title: canGetChannelData advertises zero CHANNEL_CAP -> apps may refuse CAN FD
description: canGetChannelData returns 0 for CHANNEL_CAP and CHANNEL_FLAGS (src/lib.rs:637-638). Many Kvaser apps gate features - notably CAN FD - on canCHANNELDATA_CHANNEL_CAP & canCHANNEL_CAP_CAN_FD before opening FD. With zero caps such an app concludes the channel cannot do FD even though the shim fully supports it on the wire, or treats a zero-cap channel as unusable. Latent retargeting trap.
acceptance_criteria: CHANNEL_CAP advertises plausible caps matching reality (EXTENDED_CAN | CAN_FD | VIRTUAL at minimum). FD-gating apps see an FD-capable channel.
close_reason: canGetChannelData CHANNEL_CAP now advertises EXTENDED_CAN|VIRTUAL|CAN_FD so FD-gating apps enable FD.

title: UDP ephemeral-port fallback silently yields a TX-only channel (RX dead)
description: bind_udp (transport.rs:240-248) falls back to an OS-assigned port on AddrInUse (kvasilloni-iai) and canOpenChannel reports success with only a log line. But cannelloni ALWAYS sendto the fixed -R/-r address and never learns the source port (verified: refs/cannelloni/udpthread.cpp:295 + parsePacket :104-110). So after a fallback cannelloni keeps sending to the original -r port, which the shim is no longer bound to -> RX is silently dead. The app sees a successful open and working canWrite, with frames that never arrive. The iai close-note rationale ('cannelloni learns the peer from received datagrams') is factually wrong. The --multi selftest tacitly accepts this (only checks RX on the first channel, probe line 276).
acceptance_criteria: Default behavior no longer silently produces an RX-dead channel: either fail the open with a clear, actionable message, or make the ephemeral fallback explicitly opt-in (config/env) with the TX-only caveat documented. Update README/ini.example/config doc and the --multi probe/selftest accordingly.
close_reason: UDP ephemeral fallback is now opt-in (udp_port_fallback / KVASILLONI_UDP_PORT_FALLBACK, default off). Default: a busy localport fails the open with an actionable hint instead of silently producing a TX-only channel. README/ini/probe/selftest updated; --multi & --stress opt in.

title: Test: run the 64-bit (x86_64) DLL under wine64
description: The selftest probe is built i686, so wine only exercises the 32-bit DLL. The x86_64 build runtime behavior - notably the 64-bit canNotifyData layout (compile-time-asserted only), FFI marshalling, and the handle table - is never executed. Build a 64-bit probe (x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc) and run the selftest (or a subset) against the x86_64-pc-windows-gnu DLL under wine64. Retires the compile-checked-only caveat on the 64-bit build.
acceptance_criteria: Selftest can target the 64-bit DLL under wine64 (arch selectable via env/flag); core cases (UDP/TCP classic, notify reading info.rx.id) pass for x86_64.
close_reason: Parameterized selftest by SELFTEST_ARCH (32 default | 64); wine-10 runs both via wow64. Added make selftest64. Full 10-case suite (incl. --notify-close reading info.rx.id through the real 64-bit offsets) PASSES against the x86_64 DLL - runtime validation of the 64-bit build that was previously only compile-time-asserted.

title: Test: RX thread survives malformed input and keeps delivering (kkt end-to-end)
description: Unit tests prove parse_udp/decode_stream reject len>64, but nothing proves the live RX thread survives a bad datagram and continues delivering. Add a selftest step that injects a malformed UDP datagram (e.g. a frame claiming len=100) to the shim port, then a valid cansend, and asserts the valid frame still reaches canRead. Optionally a malformed-TCP variant too.
acceptance_criteria: Selftest injects garbage then a valid frame; the valid frame is delivered to canRead, proving the RX thread was not killed by the malformed input.
close_reason: Added selftest case (python3 raw UDP): injects a malformed cannelloni datagram (classic frame claiming len=100, which panicked the pre-kkt decoder) straight to the shim RX port, then a valid cansend; asserts the valid frame still reaches canRead - proving the RX thread survived. Passes 32/64-bit. Skips gracefully if python3 absent.

title: Test: canClose from a notify callback does not self-join (cqe) + notify delivers info.rx.id
description: The thread-id self-join guard in Conn::close (cqe) prevents a deadlock when an app calls canClose from inside its canSetNotify callback (which runs on the RX thread). It has NO test. The selftest callback only increments a counter and never reads the struct, so notify VALUE delivery (info.rx.id through the real Windows offsets) is also unverified. Add a --notify-close probe mode whose callback reads info.rx.id and records it, then calls canClose; the selftest asserts the process exits without hanging AND the recorded id matches the injected frame.
acceptance_criteria: A --notify-close selftest case passes under wine: process terminates (no deadlock/timeout) after canClose is called from within the notify callback, and the callback-observed info.rx.id equals the injected id.
close_reason: Added --notify-close probe mode + selftest case: the callback (on the RX thread) reads info.rx.id then calls canClose; selftest asserts 'NOTIFYCLOSE id=0x18FF0114 closed=1' - proves no self-join deadlock (cqe) AND correct notify value delivery. Passes on 32- and 64-bit DLL under wine.

title: Test coverage for Win11 hardening gaps (audit follow-up)
description: The Win11 robustness audit (kkt/hc9/cqe/uzk/iai/j83/o4z/1gl, all closed) shipped fixes whose verification partly rests on code review rather than execution. This epic groups the tasks that close those test-coverage gaps: subtle threading fixes that are unit-tested or compile-checked only, the 64-bit DLL that is never actually run, and TCP-server/concurrency paths that are unexercised. Source: testing-gap analysis from the audit session.
close_reason: All 8 children complete. The audit's code-review-only verification gaps are now executed: RX panic firewall (ehp), concurrency/stress (eoq), connect/accept timeouts + log cache + channel memo (7yl), TCP server role (6ot), per-channel RX isolation (pwx), plus the earlier cqe/kkt/64-bit cases. 39 host unit tests pass; selftest passes 32- and 64-bit with all new cases.

title: Fixed UDP localport 20000 blocks running two app instances concurrently
description: UDP bind uses cfg.local_port (default 20000) verbatim (transport.rs:212). Launching the same CAN app twice, or two CAN apps, makes the second canOpenChannel fail at bind (AddrInUse -> CAN_ERR_NOTFOUND). For a shim meant to 'just work' while inspecting arbitrary software, parallel instances are common. Fix: if binding the configured local_port fails with AddrInUse, fall back to an ephemeral port (bind 0.0.0.0:0) and log the chosen port; the cannelloni server side already learns the peer from received datagrams (or via -R/-p). Keep the explicit port honored when free.
acceptance_criteria: Second concurrent instance opens successfully on an ephemeral port (logged) instead of failing; explicit free port still used as-is.
close_reason: bind_udp() falls back to an ephemeral port on AddrInUse; actual port exposed via Conn::local_port() and logged by canOpenChannel; README updated.

title: Single global CONN breaks multi-channel apps; UDP 2nd open fails, TCP silently hijacks handle
description: State is one static CONN: Mutex<Option<Conn>> (lib.rs:51) and canOpenChannel always returns the fixed handle 1; _hnd is ignored everywhere. An app that opens more than one channel: (UDP) the 2nd canOpenChannel tries to bind the same local_port and fails with CAN_ERR_NOTFOUND; (TCP) the 2nd open succeeds and *overwrites* CONN, silently dropping the first connection while both handles still read/write the second. Threaded apps that drive one channel per thread get cross-talk or a dead first channel. Fix: keep a handle->Conn map (return distinct handles, validate _hnd), or at minimum document the single-channel limitation and fail the 2nd open deterministically with a clear log.
acceptance_criteria: Either multiple channels are independently supported with distinct validated handles, or a 2nd canOpenChannel fails fast with a logged, documented single-channel error; decided and implemented.
close_reason: Replaced single global CONN with a BTreeMap<handle,Conn> + atomic handle allocator. canOpenChannel returns a distinct non-negative handle per channel (first still =1, backward-compatible); every op resolves its hnd and returns canERR_INVHANDLE(-10) for unknown handles; canClose removes from the map outside the lock; close is a lenient no-op for unknown handles. Combined with the iai ephemeral fallback, one process can open multiple UDP channels. Validated: new unit test (invalid-handle paths) + new --multi probe mode and selftest case (two channels, distinct handles, both TX seen on vcan, first channel still RX) PASS under wine.

title: RX background thread has no panic firewall (any panic kills RX permanently)
description: udp_rx_loop and tcp_rx_loop (transport.rs:377,398) decode untrusted network bytes and call rx.push() (which also invokes the app notify callback) with no catch_unwind. Any panic on this thread - the overlong-length bug, a future decoder change, or a panicking app notify callback - terminates the only RX thread with no log and no recovery; canRead then returns NOMSG forever. Defense in depth on top of input validation. Fix: wrap the per-iteration decode+push in catch_unwind (log and continue), or at minimum guarantee the loop cannot panic.
acceptance_criteria: A panic inside frame decode or the notify callback is caught, logged, and the RX loop continues; covered by a test that pushes a frame whose notify callback panics.
close_reason: RX loops wrap the untrusted decode/enqueue path in catch_unwind (UDP parse+push, TCP decode_stream). Documented that a panic inside the app's extern callback aborts at the FFI boundary by Rust's rules.

title: canbridge.py: harden for multi-app/multi-thread use (FD, error resilience, loop guard)
description: Audit of new userspace CAN bridge found: (1) classic-only sockets silently drop CAN FD frames (project is FD-heavy); (2) pump threads have no try/except so any transient send/recv error silently kills one direction; (3) duplicate bridges on the same iface-pair create an infinite amplification loop; (4) blocking send couples directions and stalls shutdown; (5) bare ImportError; (6) partial-open socket leak; (7) no observability. Apply: fd=True, resilient pump w/ counters + transition logging, flock per-pair loop guard (--force override), send timeout, friendly import error, partial-open cleanup, optional --stats heartbeat + final summary.
close_reason: Verified already fixed in committed code (the 'moved from gist' version is the hardened one). Confirmed all 7 fixes live on real vcan: classic+FD forwarding both directions; loop-guard refuses duplicate (either order); self-bridge rejected; --force override; fault resilience (iface down->up recovers both directions); --stats heartbeat + final summary; friendly ImportError; partial-open cleanup. Per-uid lock scope left as-is per user (single-user host).

title: GitHub Actions release CI: tag-triggered Windows DLL builds (32/64-bit) + release with sha256sums
description: On pushing a v* tag, cross-compile canlib32.dll for i686 and x86_64 windows-gnu targets, attach both DLLs plus SHA256SUMS.txt to a GitHub release. Repo: aalarchiv/kvasilloni. Cut v0.2.0.
close_reason: Done: release.yml implements the tag-triggered Windows DLL build + GitHub release with sha256sums; it has shipped v0.2.0 and v0.3.0 (both arch zips + SHA256SUMS.txt). Feature complete.

title: Replace non-ASCII chars in docs/sources (em-dashes, arrows, box-drawing)
description: Project docs and Rust/C sources use UTF-8 punctuation (em/en dashes, arrows, ellipsis, middle dot, box-drawing glyphs) that may not render for readers without full UTF-8 support. Replace with plain ASCII equivalents in our own files; leave vendored .beads/ and license COPYING* untouched.
close_reason: Replaced em/en dashes, arrows, ellipsis, approx, middle dot, and box-drawing glyphs with ASCII across our docs and sources; vendored .beads/ and COPYING* left untouched; tests pass.

title: CAN-FD through canWrite + INI config
description: Wire CAN-FD send (FDF/BRS/ESI, 64-byte payloads, DLC rounding) and add Windows-native INI config (kvasilloni.ini, env override).
close_reason: Done. canWrite FD path (fd_round_len + BRS/ESI mapping), canRead reports FDF/BRS/ESI. INI config via src/config.rs (defaults<ini<env), auto-discovered next to DLL/exe via GetModuleFileNameW. 11 unit tests + e2e selftest PASS x3 (classic UDP/TCP, FD+BRS over UDP, INI-only no-env).

title: README + Windows-VM deployment docs
description: Architecture diagram, build, env vars, both transports, cannelloni CLI for each side, drop-in deploy steps.
close_reason: README written: architecture, build, Windows deploy + env vars, cannelloni CLI for UDP/TCP, selftest, wire-format reference.

title: wine + vcan end-to-end selftest
description: Build cannelloni from refs, bring up vcan0, run cannelloni (UDP and TCP), run canshim_probe.exe under wine, assert frames cross both directions. Skip gracefully without root/wine.
close_reason: Rust shim e2e selftest PASS: both UDP and TCP, both directions, through wine + stock cannelloni + isolated vcan1. Found cannelloni TCP server needs -R or -p (peer check).

title: Makefile: 32+64-bit DLL build, export verify
description: Build i686 and x86_64 canlib32.dll via mingw with --kill-at undecorated stdcall exports; targets: all, test, verify (objdump exports), selftest.
close_reason: Makefile builds both bitnesses with undecorated exports; make verify confirms all 13 names, no @N decoration

title: R5: config.rs INI/env robustness (property/fuzz the layered parser)
description:
PROBLEM
config.rs (layered defaults < kvasilloni.ini < KVASILLONI_* env, parse_num
warnings, IPv6 host, allow-list) was NOT in im6's scope. It has unit tests for
the cache/memo helpers but no systematic coverage of the parse/validation matrix,
and it feeds security-relevant settings (peer_check, allow-list).

INSTRUCTIONS
- Property-test apply_map / apply_env (factor them to take an injected map/env if
  needed so tests don't mutate process env and race): for arbitrary key/value
  maps, Config building never panics; numeric keys (port/localport/channels/
  connecttimeout/accepttimeout) that are out-of-range or non-numeric keep the
  default AND push a warning; blank values are silently ignored (no warning).
- Host parsing: valid IPv4 and IPv6 literals are accepted; an empty/garbage host
  warns and keeps the default (and is later rejected at Conn::connect).
- allow-list: comma/space-separated IPs parse into the expected Vec<IpAddr>;
  junk entries are handled per the documented behavior.
- Toggle mapping: proto t* -> tcp, tcprole s* -> server, peer_check off/0/no etc.
  map to the right booleans (case-insensitive).

ACCEPTANCE
- The parse/validation matrix is covered; warnings asserted on the bad-value paths.
- Removing a validation (e.g. the u16 port parse/warning) fails a test.
- No test mutates global process env in a way that races other parallel tests.
acceptance_criteria: Property/fuzz coverage of Config parsing: arbitrary INI maps and KVASILLONI_* env values never panic; out-of-range/garbage numerics keep the default AND record a warning; valid IPv4/IPv6 hosts and allow-lists parse; the proto/tcprole/peer_check toggles map correctly. A reverted validation (e.g. dropping the port range/warning) is caught.

title: R4: Error-path resource-leak audit (failed opens leak no fd/thread)
description:
PROBLEM
im6.5 covers the TCP write-error teardown, but the partial-OPEN failure paths
have no leak coverage: a Conn::connect that binds/connects then fails the
handshake, or a bind that fails, must not strand a socket or an RX thread. The
stress test checks the CONNS map for leaks but not OS-level fd/thread leaks.

INSTRUCTIONS (loopback, no vcan/cannelloni)
- Handshake-fail leak: stand up a loopback TCP server that accepts but sends a
  WRONG banner (reuse im6.5's pattern), point a client Conn::connect at it, and
  assert it returns Err. Repeat N times; assert the process fd count and thread
  count do not grow (read /proc/self/fd and /proc/self/task on Linux, or a
  before/after delta with a tolerance).
- Bind-fail: occupy a TCP server port, attempt a server-role Conn::connect on it
  (fallback off), assert Err and no leaked listener/thread.
- If Conn::connect spawns the RX thread only AFTER negotiation, confirm the
  failure path never spawns it (no orphan thread); if it can, confirm it is
  joined/stopped on the error return.

ACCEPTANCE
- N failed opens leave fd/thread counts flat (within tolerance).
- Reverting a cleanup (e.g. skip socket shutdown on handshake error) fails it.
acceptance_criteria: A loopback test drives many FAILED opens - TCP connect-OK-but-handshake-fail and UDP/TCP bind-fail - and asserts no growth in open fds or live threads afterward. Reverting a cleanup path (e.g. not joining/closing on a handshake error) makes the test fail.
close_reason:
Added two Linux-only loopback leak tests to transport.rs covering the partial-open failure paths im6.5 did not:
- failed_tcp_handshake_opens_leak_no_fd_or_thread: a loopback server accepts then sends a WRONG banner; 100 client Conn::connect attempts fail the handshake AFTER connecting. Confirms no fd/thread growth (the connected socket + handshake clone are dropped; the RX thread is never spawned - handshake runs before the spawn).
- failed_bind_opens_leak_no_fd_or_thread: server-role connect onto a busy port fails with AddrInUse; 100 attempts leak no listener fd or thread.
Counts read /proc/self/fd and /proc/self/task. Since those are process-global and parallel tests add noise, the tests flag only GROWTH and tolerate N/4 - far above neighbour noise, far below the ~N a real per-iteration leak produces (a genuine leak scales with N, noise does not).
VERIFIED revert-detection: adding a std::mem::forget(clone) on the handshake error path makes the test fail (fd 6->104 over 99, tol 24). Stable across repeated full-suite runs (84 tests green).

title: R2: cargo-fuzz harness for parse_udp + decode_stream
description:
PROBLEM
The untrusted parse surface has no fuzzing. Property tests (R1) sample structured
inputs; a coverage-guided fuzzer explores byte-level edge cases (truncations,
flag combinations, count/len boundaries) that structured generators miss.

INSTRUCTIONS
- Add a `fuzz/` cargo-fuzz workspace with two targets:
    fuzz_target!(|data: &[u8]| { let _ = wire::parse_udp(data); });
    a second target that drives decode_stream over `data` in need-sized chunks.
  Both must exercise the REAL functions (expose them via a small pub(crate) shim
  or a fuzz feature if needed; do not duplicate logic).
- Build with ASAN (the cargo-fuzz default) so any OOB/UB aborts.
- Document `cargo fuzz run parse_udp` / `decode_stream` and a seed corpus (a few
  golden datagrams from wire.rs tests) in the repo.
- Optionally wire a short (e.g. 60s) fuzz smoke into CI if/when CI exists.

NETWORK CAVEAT
cargo-fuzz + a nightly toolchain are network/installed-tooling dependent. If
unavailable on this host, record that clearly and lean on R1; do not block the
epic on R2.

ACCEPTANCE
- Both fuzz targets build + run clean under ASAN; recipe documented.
- (If tooling unavailable) issue documents the blocker and defers to R1.
acceptance_criteria: fuzz/ targets for parse_udp and decode_stream build and run clean under ASAN on a nightly toolchain; a documented 'cargo fuzz run' recipe is in the repo (README or test/). A short corpus run finds no panic/UB. If the host cannot install cargo-fuzz/nightly, the issue records that and defers to R1's property tests.

title: Add FFI-boundary tests for under-tested exports (canReadStatus, CHANNEL_CAP, ...)
description:
Add FFI-boundary tests for exports currently hit only on the error path (or not
at all).

PROBLEM
Several src/lib.rs exports are only exercised via the INVHANDLE/error branch or
not unit-tested: canReadStatus (RX_PENDING/SW_OVERRUN mapping), canGetChannelData
CHANNEL_CAP (the FD-capability mask apps gate FD on), canGetErrorText (truncation
/ NUL / unknown-err), canReadWait/canReadSync success path + the kvasilloni-2qh
read-inside-notify non-blocking behavior, canIoCtl GET_RX_BUFFER_LEVEL (func 8)
success, and RTR end-to-end.

INSTRUCTIONS (lib.rs #[cfg(test)]; open a real Conn over loopback UDP - cfg!(test)
enables ephemeral fallback - and inject frames via conn.rx_shared().push(); push
is pub on Shared)
- canReadStatus: open, assert 0; push a frame -> assert RX_PENDING; push past
  RING_CAP -> assert SW_OVERRUN; bad handle -> flags 0 / CAN_OK (lenient).
- canIoCtl func 8 (GET_RX_BUFFER_LEVEL): push N frames, assert it writes N; func
  10 (FLUSH_RX) then drains to 0.
- canReadWait: push a frame -> returns it (assert id/dlc/data); empty -> NOMSG.
- canGetChannelData item 1 (CHANNEL_CAP): assert CAN_FD|EXTENDED_CAN|VIRTUAL bits.
- canGetErrorText: into a 4-byte buffer -> truncated + NUL-terminated; bufsiz=0 ->
  buffer untouched; unknown err code -> "Unknown error".
- RTR: a lib.rs unit test for write_frame RTR (skips data) and write_read_outputs
  RTR path (no data copy, dlc reported); optionally a probe rtr mode + selftest
  case for true e2e.

ACCEPTANCE
Each listed export has a test asserting exact success/edge values (not just a
non-crash). canReadStatus overrun + CHANNEL_CAP FD bits + canGetErrorText
truncation are covered.
close_reason: Added 7 FFI-boundary tests in lib.rs (push made pub on Shared): canReadStatus pending+overrun+lenient-bad-handle, canIoCtl GET_RX_BUFFER_LEVEL+FLUSH_RX, canReadWait success(id/dlc/data)+NOMSG, canGetChannelData CHANNEL_CAP FD/EXT/VIRTUAL bits, canGetErrorText truncation/NUL/bufsiz0/unknown-err, write_read_outputs RTR (verified attributable), canWrite RTR e2e datagram. Found+fixed a real test-isolation flake: added CONNS_TEST_LOCK serializing all channel-owning tests against the stress test's CONNS-empty assertion + :20000 stray RX. 74 tests, 12x flake-checked green.

title: Add fast integration tests for the transport.rs network layer
description:
Add fast (cargo test) integration tests for the transport.rs network layer.

PROBLEM
All ~19 transport.rs tests exercise Shared (the ring) or pure helper fns. The
real network paths have ZERO fast coverage - regressions only surface in the slow
wine/cannelloni selftest. Use loopback 127.0.0.1 sockets; no vcan/cannelloni/wine
needed. cfg!(test) already force-enables the UDP ephemeral fallback.

INSTRUCTIONS (add as #[cfg(test)] in src/transport.rs; checklist)
- udp_rx_loop: bind two loopback UdpSockets; spawn the loop on one; send an
  allowed datagram (build_udp) and one from a disallowed source; assert the ring
  receives only the allowed frame; set running=false and join.
- tcp_rx_loop: a loopback TcpStream pair; write one encode_frame output in 1-byte
  chunks; assert the reassembled frame is pushed (covers split-across-reads). Then
  feed bytes that decode to Decoded::Error; assert the loop exits.
- Conn::connect (TCP): bring up a server-role and client-role Conn over loopback
  and exchange one frame end-to-end; also assert a non-IP host string -> Err.
- Conn::write TCP write-error teardown (NO coverage anywhere today): connect, drop
  the peer, write() -> assert Err AND is_ready()==false (negotiated/running
  cleared, socket shut down).
- handshake: peer sends 12 wrong bytes -> assert Err (InvalidData).
- accept_with_timeout: listener + a disallowed connect followed by an allowed
  connect -> returns the allowed peer; with no client -> TimedOut.
- bind_udp: occupy a port, call bind_udp(same_addr, fallback=true) -> a different
  non-zero port.

ACCEPTANCE
- All new tests run under `cargo test` (fast, no external deps) and each fails if
  its production path regresses. In particular the write-error teardown path is
  now covered.
close_reason: Added 10 fast loopback network tests in transport.rs: udp_rx_loop peer-filter (two source IPs 127.0.0.1/127.0.0.2), tcp_rx_loop split-write reassembly + exit-on-Error, Conn TCP server<->client frame exchange both directions, Conn::connect non-IP host Err, TCP write-error teardown (is_ready cleared - verified attributable), handshake wrong-banner InvalidData, accept_with_timeout allowed/disallowed-then-timeout/no-client, bind_udp ephemeral fallback. 67 tests total, 8x flake-checked green.

title: Strengthen 5 unit tests that pass even when their behavior is broken
description:
Replace/strengthen unit tests that pass even when their target behavior is broken.

Fix each of the following so the test FAILS if the named behavior regresses
(verify by temporarily reverting the relevant production code, then restore):

a) parse_udp_huge_count_does_not_over_allocate (src/wire.rs ~:597) - MISLEADING.
   The returned None comes from the per-frame bounds check, not the allocation
   cap (wire.rs ~:294); deleting the cap line still passes. Either rename it to
   what it actually asserts (graceful None on a huge count, no panic) AND add a
   separate test attributable to the cap - e.g. craft a datagram whose `count` is
   large but which carries a few real frame bodies, parse it, and assert the
   returned Vec length matches the real bodies (and capacity is bounded) - or
   drop the misleading name.

b) set_notify_reentrant_does_not_deadlock (src/transport.rs ~:953) - WEAK. It
   pokes the IN_NOTIFY_CB thread-local directly. Instead drive a REAL callback
   that calls set_notify(0,0,0) on its own Shared from inside the callback
   (reach the Shared via a static raw pointer/usize set up by the test), pushed
   through Shared::push so CbScope is actually entered. Assert push returns (no
   hang) and the notify was disarmed.

c) push_stamps_receive_timestamp (src/transport.rs ~:803) - WEAK (only asserts
   >0). Tighten: t0 = now_ms() before push, t1 = now_ms() after; assert
   t0 <= frame.rx_time_ms <= t1.

d) number_of_channels_defaults_to_one (src/lib.rs ~:820) - WEAK. Assert == 1, not
   >= 1 (no other test sets ENUM_CHANNELS, so == 1 is safe).

e) notify_data_field_order (src/transport.rs ~:1030) - WEAK proxy on host. Low
   value: the real ABI guard is the #[cfg(windows)] const assert (transport.rs
   ~:57). Add a comment pointing at that guard; optionally also assert the host
   struct field offsets. Leaving it is acceptable once documented.

ACCEPTANCE
Each rewritten test (a-d) fails when its production logic is reverted and passes
when restored; (e) carries a comment naming the real compile-time guard.
close_reason: a) renamed parse_udp_huge_count_*_does_not_over_allocate -> *_no_bodies_is_graceful_none + added udp_prealloc_cap helper & udp_prealloc_cap_clamps_attacker_count (attributable to the cap). b) set_notify_reentrant now drives a real cb through push() (CbScope entered); verified it deadlocks (timeout 124) without the IN_NOTIFY_CB skip. c) push_stamps_receive_timestamp brackets the stamp in [t0,t1]; verified fails when stamp reverted to 0. d) number_of_channels assert ==1; verified fails under KVASILLONI_CHANNELS=2. e) notify_data_field_order comment now names the #[cfg(windows)] const-assert guard.

title: Blocking canReadWait/canReadSync inside a notify callback stalls the RX thread
description: The canSetNotify callback fires on the RX/producer thread. A callback that calls canReadWait/canReadSync on the same handle blocks the only producer in its own consumer wait -- returns immediately while frames are queued, then parks for the full timeout once drained (forever with canWAIT_INFINITE, until close()). Real Kvaser fires notify on a separate driver thread, so this diverges. App footgun; document the constraint (don't block inside the callback) and/or note in README. Found via adversarial review 2026-06-26.
close_reason: Added transport::in_notify_callback() (reads the IN_NOTIFY_CB thread-local from the 4vc fix). canReadWait/canReadSync now use read_wait_duration(): a blocking read issued from inside a notify callback (on the RX thread) collapses to a non-blocking poll instead of stalling the only producer. README caveat updated.

title: Classic-RTR DLC dropped on TCP decode but kept on UDP (transport asymmetry)
description: wire.rs decode_stream zeroes f.len for a classic RTR frame; parse_udp keeps the on-wire DLC. Same RTR frame yields dlc=0 over TCP, original DLC over UDP. Mirrors an inconsistency in upstream cannelloni (decoder.cpp vs parser.cpp) so it is 'faithful', but it is an observable behavioral difference between transports. FD-RTR is consistent (kept) in both. Low severity. Found via adversarial review 2026-06-26.
close_reason: decode_stream (TCP) now keeps the classic-RTR DLC instead of zeroing it, matching parse_udp (UDP); the <=8 length check moved ahead of the RTR branch so RTR is bounds-checked too. New test classic_rtr_dlc_consistent_across_transports. Both transports now report the same RTR DLC.

title: Config silently ignores empty host and out-of-range numerics
description: config.rs: KVASILLONI_HOST="" / host= overwrites the default with empty (then canOpenChannel fails IP parse -> NOTFOUND); port/localport (u16) and connect/accept timeouts + channels use .parse().ok() so port=70000 silently keeps the default rather than signalling misconfig. Validate/clamp and log a warning so misconfiguration is visible. Low severity. Found via adversarial review 2026-06-26.
close_reason: config.rs: empty host (ini or KVASILLONI_HOST) no longer clobbers the default; present-but-unparseable numerics (port/localport/channels/connecttimeout/accepttimeout) now record a warning via parse_num() and keep the default instead of silently falling back. Config.warnings is logged by canOpenChannel. New test invalid_values_warn_and_keep_defaults.

title: Several extern "system" exports lack the catch_unwind guard
description: canSetBusParams, canBusOn, canBusOff, canReadErrorCounters, canGetBusStatistics, canGetErrorText, canFlushTransmitQueue, canSetBusOutputControl, canWriteSync, canObjBufSetFilter, canInitializeLibrary run their body without guard(). No current path panics (log() swallows all errors), so latent only -- but a future edit could unwind across the FFI boundary (process abort) instead of returning an error code. Wrap them in guard() like their peers for defense in depth. Found via adversarial review 2026-06-26.
close_reason: Wrapped the previously-unguarded extern "system" exports in guard()/guard_void() (canInitializeLibrary, canSetBusParams, canBusOn/Off, canReadErrorCounters, canGetBusStatistics, canGetErrorText, canFlushTransmitQueue, canSetBusOutputControl, canWriteSync, canObjBufSetFilter) so a future panic can't unwind across the FFI boundary. Added guard_void for the ()-returning canInitializeLibrary.

title: IPv6 support: shim is IPv4-only (binds 0.0.0.0; host parse lacks IPv6 brackets)
description: transport.rs binds 0.0.0.0 for UDP and the TCP listener (IPv4 wildcard only), and connect() builds the remote with format!("{}:{}", host, port) which cannot parse a bracketless IPv6 literal (e.g. fe80::1) into a SocketAddr -> 'bad host'. So an IPv6 cannelloni peer cannot be used, even though cannelloni itself supports AF_INET6. Fix without new deps: parse cfg.host as IpAddr, build remote via SocketAddr::new(ip, port), and bind the unspecified address of the matching family (0.0.0.0 for v4, :: for v6). Peer-check compares IpAddr so it is family-agnostic.
acceptance_criteria: Configuring an IPv6 host works for UDP and TCP (client/server): the shim binds the matching family and connects/accepts over IPv6. IPv4 default (127.0.0.1) unchanged. Documented.
close_reason: host parsed as IpAddr (v4/v6); bind unspecified addr of peer family (0.0.0.0 / ::). IPv6 hosts now work for UDP + TCP.

title: canRead/canReadWait/notify timestamps are always 0
description: canRead/canReadWait set *time=0 (lib.rs:302-303) and the notify struct carries time=0 (transport.rs:140). cannelloni's wire format carries no timestamp, but the shim can stamp a monotonic receive timestamp at ingest. Apps doing latency/ordering/replay analysis currently get nothing. GET_TIMER_SCALE already reports 1000 us/tick, so ms ticks are consistent.
acceptance_criteria: RX frames carry a monotonic receive timestamp (ms) stamped at ingest; canRead/canReadWait/notify report it in Kvaser timer units consistent with GET_TIMER_SCALE.
close_reason: Frame.rx_time_ms stamped at RX ingest (now_ms via OnceLock<Instant>); canRead/canReadWait/notify report it in ms, consistent with GET_TIMER_SCALE=1000.

title: parse_udp pre-allocates from attacker-controlled count before validating length
description: parse_udp does Vec::with_capacity(count as usize) with an attacker-controlled u16 count BEFORE validating against the datagram length (src/wire.rs:271). A 7-byte spoofed datagram forces a ~5MB allocation (count=65535 * sizeof Frame). Bounded and freed, but a cheap allocation-amplification / memory-churn vector, reachable by anyone who can hit the 0.0.0.0-bound UDP port. Related: the RX socket ignores the source address (transport.rs:510), so it is also an open injection surface (cannelloni peer-checks by default; the shim does not).
acceptance_criteria: Pre-allocation capped to what the datagram could possibly hold (buf.len()/FRAME_BASE_SIZE). Optionally document the open UDP injection surface / consider binding a specific local address.
close_reason: parse_udp caps Vec::with_capacity to buf.len()/FRAME_BASE_SIZE; spoofed huge count no longer forces a multi-MB allocation. Injection-surface half handled by kvasilloni-872 (peer check).

title: RX ring overflow drops frames silently with no counter or status flag
description: Shared::push drops frames when the ring is full (transport.rs:124-126) with no counter and no overrun signal, and canReadStatus/canReadErrorCounters are hardcoded to healthy/zero (lib.rs:321-349). An app that falls behind loses data invisibly with no API surface to detect it.
acceptance_criteria: Track an overflow latch on the ring; surface it via canReadStatus (canSTAT_SW_OVERRUN/RX_PENDING). Overrun observable by the app and cleared on flush.
close_reason: Shared.overflow AtomicBool latches ring-full drops; canReadStatus reports canSTAT_SW_OVERRUN + RX_PENDING; cleared on flush. Unit + behavior tested.

title: Test: per-channel RX isolation (two cannelloni endpoints)
description: The --multi selftest only polls channel A (cannelloni replies to the configured port), so per-channel RX queue isolation is unverified - we do not prove a frame for channel B lands in B queue and not A. Add a test with two cannelloni instances on distinct ports so each shim channel has its own RX path, inject a distinct frame to each, and assert each canRead returns only its own channel frame.
acceptance_criteria: Two-endpoint multi-channel test: frames injected to each cannelloni instance are delivered only to the corresponding channel canRead, with no cross-channel leakage.
close_reason: Added --multi-rx probe mode that opens two channels to two distinct cannelloni endpoints by reloading config (SetEnvironmentVariableA) between opens, since the shim reads config per canOpenChannel. New selftest case runs two cannelloni on isolated vcan1/vcan2; injects a distinct frame on each and asserts each channel's canRead sees only its own frame (no cross-channel leak). Passes 32- and 64-bit.

title: Test: concurrency/stress (multi-thread handles, rapid open/close, RX under load)
description: No test exercises true concurrent use: multiple threads driving different handles at once, rapid canOpenChannel/canClose cycles, or RX under sustained frame load. The handle table is mutex-serialized but unproven under contention; close()/RX-thread teardown races (hc9/cqe) are only unit-tested in isolation. Add a stress probe/test that opens N channels across threads, hammers read/write, and closes concurrently, asserting no deadlock/crash and no leaked handles/threads.
acceptance_criteria: A stress run (multiple threads, many open/close + read/write cycles) completes without deadlock, crash, or handle/thread leak.
close_reason: Added host-side cargo test stress_concurrent_open_write_read_close (8 threads x 6 open/close cycles x 40 write/read) asserting no deadlock/crash and an empty handle table afterwards (no leak). Also added a wine-side --stress probe mode + selftest case (timeout-bounded so a deadlock fails rather than hangs) exercising the shipped DLL under real Windows threads. Passes 32- and 64-bit.

title: Test: automate the 1gl checks (connect-timeout fast-fail, log cache, channels memo)
description: Several 1gl behaviors are unautomated: (a) connect-timeout fast-failure was verified by hand (blackhole host, ~1.5s vs ~5s); (b) log() file-handle cache (path-change reopen, open-failure fallback) has no test; (c) canGetNumberOfChannels memoization (read-once) is untested and silently changes runtime re-read behavior. Add a selftest timing assertion for the connect timeout, and unit/integration tests for the log cache and channel memo (refactor log() to be path-injectable if needed to avoid env-mutation races under parallel tests).
acceptance_criteria: Automated checks cover: connect timeout shortens open failure to ~the configured value; log writes reuse the cached handle and a path change reopens; channel count is read once and returned consistently.
close_reason: Automated all three 1gl checks. (a) connect-timeout fast-fail: --timed-open probe vs a silent TCP listener, selftest asserts open fails in ~connecttimeout (1502ms) not the 5s default. (b) log cache: log() split into path-injectable log_to(); unit tests cover handle reuse (unlink-while-open proof), path-change reopen, and open-failure fallback - no env mutation. (c) channels memo: extracted channels_memo(cell, loader); unit test proves the loader runs once (read-once).

title: Test or document: uzk RX panic firewall actually contains a panic
description: With kkt fixed, nothing in the RX decode/push path panics, so the catch_unwind firewall (uzk) never triggers in tests - it is unverified. Either add a test-only fault-injection hook (cfg(test) or a feature) that forces a panic in the wrapped closure and asserts the RX loop logs-and-continues, or explicitly document the firewall as intentionally-unverifiable defense-in-depth and stop counting it as tested.
acceptance_criteria: Either a test proves the RX loop survives an injected panic in the decode/push closure, or a code comment + issue note records the deliberate decision not to test it.
close_reason: Refactored the RX panic firewall into testable guarded helpers (guarded_udp_ingest / guarded_decode_stream) shared by the loops, with a #[cfg(test)] one-shot panic-injection hook (rx_panic_hook, compiled out of release). Two unit tests prove an injected panic is contained and the UDP path keeps delivering afterwards (transport.rs rx_firewall_contains_injected_panic_udp/_tcp).

title: Test: TCP server role (tcprole=server) + accepttimeout
description: The selftest always runs the shim as TCP client; the shim TCP server path (accept_with_timeout, the new accepttimeout) is entirely untested - a pre-existing gap. Add a selftest case with KVASILLONI_TCPROLE=server (shim listens, cannelloni connects as client) covering both data directions, plus a check that a server open with no client times out per accepttimeout.
acceptance_criteria: Selftest TCP-server case passes (shim accepts cannelloni connection, both directions work) and a no-client open returns an error within ~accepttimeout.
close_reason: Added TCP server-role selftest case (shim listens via KVASILLONI_TCPROLE=server, cannelloni -C c connects; both TX and RX verified) and a no-client accept-timeout case (--timed-open server, asserts open fails in ~accepttimeout=1500ms, h<0). Passes 32- and 64-bit.

title: selftest: set vcan mtu 72 so the CAN FD case can inject (cansend needs FD MTU)
description: test/selftest.sh creates vcan1 with 'ip link add ... type vcan' and brings it up, but never sets mtu 72 (CANFD_MTU). cansend '##' (FD) then fails with 'CAN interface is not CAN FD capable' and the UDP (CAN FD + BRS) case FAILs on a fresh interface, masking real FD regressions. Confirmed: setting 'ip link set vcan1 mtu 72' makes the FD case PASS (whole selftest goes green). Fix: set mtu 72 in the interface bring-up before 'set up'.
acceptance_criteria: A fresh selftest run (no pre-existing vcan1) passes the CAN FD case without manual MTU tweaking.
close_reason: selftest sets vcan mtu 72 before bring-up; fresh-interface selftest now passes the CAN FD case (verified by deleting+recreating vcan1).

title: Doc/robustness: absolute paths for log/INI, slow blocking canOpenChannel, 64-bit notify layout
description: Grab-bag of lower-severity Win11 'just works' items found in audit: (1) A relative 'log=' value or KVASILLONI_INI env resolves against the process CWD (often C:\Windows\System32 for services) not the DLL/exe dir; the INI auto-discovery itself is robust via module path, but document that log/INI paths should be absolute. (2) canOpenChannel can block the caller up to 10s (TCP connect_timeout) / 30s (TCP server accept) on the calling thread - a UI-thread open freezes the app / trips watchdogs; consider shortening or documenting. (3) canSetNotify's NotifyData/canNotifyData layout is only reasoned for the 32-bit target; verify field offsets for the shipped x86_64 (LLP64) build. (4) Minor perf: log() re-opens the file every call and canGetNumberOfChannels/canGetChannelData re-read the INI from disk each call.
acceptance_criteria: Each sub-item is either fixed or explicitly documented as a known limitation in README/AGENTS.
notes: PARTIAL (2026-06-25): absolute-path requirement for log=/KVASILLONI_INI documented in src/config.rs module doc + README; UDP ephemeral-fallback noted in README. REMAINING: (2) slow blocking canOpenChannel (10s/30s), (3) verify 64-bit NotifyData/canNotifyData layout, (4) log() reopen + Config::load() disk re-read perf.
close_reason: Full hardening pass. (1) absolute-path docs (done earlier). (2) Notify ABI: VERIFIED correct against real canlib.h (Windows+Linux) - canNotifyData.info.rx is {long id; unsigned long time;}, and Rust's Windows c_long/c_ulong are 32-bit, so the existing NotifyData matches on both targets; was NOT a bug. Locked it with a #[cfg(windows)] compile-time offset/size assertion (enforced by both cross-builds) + host-safe field-order test. (3) Configurable TCP timeouts: connecttimeout(5000, lowered from 10000)/accepttimeout(30000) via INI+env, plumbed through Conn::connect and handshake; documented that canOpenChannel blocks. Verified: KVASILLONI_CONNECT_TIMEOUT=1000 fails open in ~1.5s vs ~5s default. (4) Perf: log() caches the append File handle keyed by path; canGetNumberOfChannels memoizes channel count via OnceLock. 32 host tests + 8-case wine selftest green; 27 exports intact.

title: Implement channel enumeration: canGetNumberOfChannels / canGetChannelData
description:
Parent epic: kvasilloni-5yp (read its ONBOARDING first). HARDEST in the set.

Signatures:
  canStatus __stdcall canGetNumberOfChannels(int *channelCount);
  canStatus __stdcall canGetChannelData(int channel, int item, void *buffer, size_t bufsize);

WHY: apps that SCAN/LIST adapters (instead of opening a fixed channel from the UI) call these. The current app does NOT (PGN_TRANSPORT.md 1.1), so this is purely for retargeting.

DIRECTIONS:
- Decide the channel model. Simplest: expose a fixed channel count from config (e.g. KVASILLONI_CHANNELS / ini `channels`, default 1) so canOpenChannel(channel) maps to host vcan<channel> per existing convention.
- canGetNumberOfChannels: write that count to *channelCount, return canOK.
- canGetChannelData: switch on `item` (canCHANNELDATA_* enum in canlib.h). Implement at least:
    canCHANNELDATA_CHANNEL_NAME (string), canCHANNELDATA_DEVDESCR_ASCII, canCHANNELDATA_CARD_SERIAL_NO,
    canCHANNELDATA_CHANNEL_CAP / canCHANNELDATA_CARD_TYPE as needed.
  Fill `buffer` (bufsize-bounded) with synthetic-but-plausible values (e.g. name "kvasilloni vcan{channel}"). Return canOK for handled items, canERR_PARAM for unhandled.
- Pull the exact item enum + expected buffer types from refs/kvaser_canlib/.../canlib.h (search canCHANNELDATA_) and refs/pycanlib/canlib/canlib/enums.py (ChannelData).
- Add both names to Makefile EXPORTS.
ACCEPTANCE: make verify + make test green; a probe that enumerates prints count + a channel name. Document the item coverage in README Scope.
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement blocking I/O: canReadWait / canReadSync / canWriteWait / canWriteSync
description:
Parent epic: kvasilloni-5yp (read its ONBOARDING first).

Signatures:
  canStatus __stdcall canReadWait(CanHandle h, long *id, void *msg, unsigned *dlc, unsigned *flag, unsigned long *time, unsigned long timeout);
  canStatus __stdcall canReadSync(CanHandle h, unsigned long timeout);
  canStatus __stdcall canWriteWait(CanHandle h, long id, void *msg, unsigned dlc, unsigned flag, unsigned long timeout);
  canStatus __stdcall canWriteSync(CanHandle h, unsigned long timeout);
(timeout is in ms; 0xFFFFFFFF/canWAIT_INFINITE = block forever; 0 = poll-once.)

DIRECTIONS:
- canReadWait: like canRead but if the ring is empty, wait up to `timeout` ms for a frame, else return canERR_NOMSG. Cleanest impl: add a Condvar to the RX ring in src/transport.rs (replace Mutex<VecDeque> usage with Mutex<VecDeque>+Condvar; rx thread notify_one on push) and `read_wait(&self, Duration) -> Option<Frame>`. Keep non-blocking read() too.
- canReadSync: wait up to timeout for a frame to be AVAILABLE without removing it (peek). Return canOK if one arrives, else canERR_NOMSG.
- canWriteWait: we send synchronously already; implement as canWrite ignoring timeout (return its status). 
- canWriteSync: no TX queue -> return canOK immediately.
- Add all four names to Makefile EXPORTS.

TEST: extend test/canshim_probe.c with a `--wait` mode that calls canReadWait(timeout=2000) and assert it blocks-then-returns the injected frame; add a selftest case.
ACCEPTANCE: make verify + make test green; canReadWait returns the injected frame within timeout, canERR_NOMSG on timeout.
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement canSetBusOutputControl / canGetBusOutputControl
description:
Parent epic: kvasilloni-5yp (read its ONBOARDING first).

Signatures:
  canStatus __stdcall canSetBusOutputControl(const CanHandle hnd, const unsigned int drivertype);
  canStatus __stdcall canGetBusOutputControl(const CanHandle hnd, unsigned int *drivertype);

DIRECTIONS (benign stub, like canSetBusParams):
- Store the last drivertype in a global (static Mutex<u32> or AtomicU32 in lib.rs) on Set; return it on Get.
- canDRIVER_* constants are in canlib.h (e.g. canDRIVER_NORMAL=4, canDRIVER_SILENT=1); default to canDRIVER_NORMAL.
- Set: accept any value, store, return canOK. Get: write stored value (or NORMAL default) to *drivertype if non-null, return canOK.
- Add both names to Makefile EXPORTS.
ACCEPTANCE: make verify + make test green; Get returns what Set stored.
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement canFlushReceiveQueue / canFlushTransmitQueue
description:
Parent epic: kvasilloni-5yp (read its ONBOARDING first).

Signatures (refs/.../canlib.h):
  canStatus __stdcall canFlushReceiveQueue(const CanHandle hnd);
